Salvation is by grace through faithâ€”through belief. It has nothing to do with your own merit. You don't earn it. </i><b style="font-style: italic;">A biblical theology of belief is believing loyaltyâ€”remaining loyal to that belief.</b><i> And, of course, Christ is the object of that beliefâ€”what he did, not what you do. </i><u><i>That theology of belief does not mean we can pray a prayer of confession and then choose to follow another god or choose to follow another gospel or choose to follow no gospel at all. Belief is not uttering a prayer like it's an incantation.</i></u><span class="citation" data-citation="%7B%22citationItems%22%3A%5B%7B%22uris%22%3A%5B%22http%3A%2F%2Fzotero.org%2Fusers%2F6953150%2Fitems%2FV4WIKUF5%22%5D%2C%22itemData%22%3A%7B%22id%22%3A%22http%3A%2F%2Fzotero.org%2Fusers%2F6953150%2Fitems%2FV4WIKUF5%22%2C%22type%22%3A%22broadcast%22%2C%22abstract%22%3A%22Our%20series%20on%20the%20book%20of%20Hebrews%20continues%20the%20writer%E2%80%99s%20emphasis%20on%20the%20faithful%20priesthood%20of%20Christ%20%E2%80%93%20this%20time%20as%20the%20basis%20for%20turning%20away%20from%20a%20theology%20of%20dead%20works%20and%20clinging%20to%20faith.%20The%20centrality%20of%20not%20turning%20from%20the%20true%20gospel%20of%20faith%20in%20the%20work%20of%20Christ%20and%20God%E2%80%99s%20acceptance%20%5B%E2%80%A6%5D%22%2C%22collection-title%22%3A%22Hebrews%22%2C%22dimensions%22%3A%221%3A27%3A21%22%2C%22language%22%3A%22en-US%22%2C%22number%22%3A%22184%22%2C%22title%22%3A%22Naked%20Bible%20184%3A%20Hebrews%205%3A11-6%3A20%22%2C%22title-short%22%3A%22Naked%20Bible%20184%22%2C%22URL%22%3A%22https%3A%2F%2Fnakedbiblepodcast.com%2Fpodcast%2Fnaked-bible-184-hebrews-511-620%2F%22%2C%22author%22%3A%5B%7B%22family%22%3A%22Heiser%22%2C%22given%22%3A%22Michael%22%7D%2C%7B%22family%22%3A%22Stricklin%22%2C%22given%22%3A%22Trey%22%7D%5D%2C%22accessed%22%3A%7B%22date-parts%22%3A%5B%5B%222022%22%2C5%2C7%5D%5D%7D%7D%7D%5D%2C%22properties%22%3A%7B%7D%7D" style="font-style: italic;">(<span class="citation-item">Heiser and Stricklin</span>)</span><i>&nbsp;</i></p></blockquote><blockquote><p><i>Heiser, Michael, and Trey Stricklin. â€œ<a href="https://nakedbiblepodcast.com/podcast/naked-bible-184-hebrews-511-620/" target="_blank">Naked Bible 184: Hebrews 5:11-6:20</a>.â€ Accessed May 7, 2022.&nbsp;</i></p></blockquote></div><div><br /></div><div><br /></div><h3 style="text-align: left;">Closing Thoughts: What now?</h3><div><br /></div><div>So that's a lot of words, but hopefully you now see a different portrait for nearly every aspect of understanding "what the Bible is even about".&nbsp;</div><div><br /></div><div>Maybe I didn't convince you, but I intrigued you. If so, where do you go from here?&nbsp;</div><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">The Biblical Author's Worldview:&nbsp;</span></h4><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">The Ancient Near East (ANE) and Second Temple Judaism contexts of the biblical authors provide a window into their world, and help us make sense of often confusing stories, statements, practices, and "why it all matters".</span></div><p></p><p><span style="font-family: inherit;">When you have the biblical author's worldview in your head as you read the scrolls that we've come to call the Bible, their wisdom about life, the human condition, and how we can participate with God in his good world all come alive... at least they did for me.</span></p><p><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></p><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">Helpful Starter Resources:</span></h4><p></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: inherit;">Check out my <a href="https://www.nohiding.faith/p/resources.html" target="_blank">Resources</a> page and in&nbsp;the middle, you will find a&nbsp;</span><a href="https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/e/2PACX-1vRvRqkRH0zeT8Us2wMOa5fE3htiPxZl1ovjlCg36chFZdrUxBse0hvsQ3KMRt_gBlEjvN4LU-k6YpHj/pubhtml" target="_blank">NoHiding Resources Database (Google Sheets)</a>, which contains links to as many articles, podcasts, and videos as I could figure out how to include. I update it as I have time.</li></ul><p></p><p>If you are brand new to these ideas and this is the very first you've heard anything like this, I would suggest starting with these articles/videos:</p><p></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qwNfH_SOWKA">Compelled: Speaking and Living the Gospel - Tim Mackie (The Bible Project) - YouTube</a></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xfnqw3bnXfg">An Evening With Tom Wright 2020 (youtube.com)</a></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://www.kesherjournal.com/article/finding-our-way-through-nicaea-the-deity-of-yeshua-bilateral-ecclesiology-and-redemptive-encounter-with-the-living-god/?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTEAAR1jSmeU_7N5e_PxM3oJ-xZVBc-UqXCEgLKc8YT-Ri7XuT-TZYyOx6khA9Q_aem_AecRnZzcXnEESQcXGAU7JQNV3fExczZ44hU3ElrDaxGJRvNRdzy-_VE-6h304P2Cw9YnIGQwZ5-tlbeNQG42IBlO">Finding Our Way Through Nicaea: The Deity of Yeshua, Bilateral Ecclesiology, and Redemptive Encounter with the Living God â€“ Kesher Journal</a></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://nakedbiblepodcast.com/newstarthere/">New? Start Here! | The Naked Bible Podcast</a></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://bibleproject.com/podcasts/the-bible-project-podcast/">Bible Podcast Studying Christian Theology (bibleproject.com)</a></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLS5E_VeVNzAvrW7YsfwfpYoQ5EhU3RqRS">Ask NT Wright Anything - YouTube</a></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://www.premierunbelievable.com/shows/ask-nt-wright-anything">Ask NT Wright (premierunbelievable.com)</a></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://www.fuller.edu/womeninministry/">Women in Ministry | Fuller Seminary</a></li></ul><div><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><a href="https://www.fuller.edu/about/mission-and-values/what-we-believe-and-teach/">Our Beliefs | Our Teaching | Fuller Seminary</a>&nbsp;(Specifically, the sub-section titled "THE LANGUAGE OF â€œINERRANCYâ€ AND ITS DANGERS")</li></ul></div><div><br /></div><h3 style="text-align: left;">So, who the heck am I?</h3><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">Hi! I'm Darrell Wolfe.&nbsp;</span></h4><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">I'm Autistic &amp; ADHD (AuDHD) and Biblical Studies is my nerdy special interest. I have a BA in General Christian Studies and I make complicated biblical things simple (although I sometimes further complicate them first).&nbsp;</span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">*I replaced my former write-up in this section with my three-essay answers to my application to Fuller Theological Seminary to their <a href="https://www.fuller.edu/master-of-arts-in-theological-studies-degree-outline/" target="_blank">Masters in Theological Studies (MTS)</a>, biblical emphasis.&nbsp;</span></div><div><br /></div><div><h4 style="text-align: left;">Darrell's Religious Autobiography&nbsp;</h4><div>I started life as a pastorâ€™s kid (PK); the son of an alcoholic pastor turned atheist crack-addict psychologist. Mike Wolfe had an MDiv and DMin from Claremont School of Theology and we pastored in the Disciples of Christ (DOC). While living in a largely Cessationist tradition, I grew up having both seen Jesus and an entity of the enemy by around five years old, in addition to other spiritual dreams and experiences. Those left me with cognitive dissonance and questions. I spent time in various traditions seeking to understand what was true; but I ran from the word â€œpastorâ€ and from bible school.</div><div><br /></div><div>In 2003 I met my now late-wife and began a journey through the world of Pentecostals and Word of Faith, serving Kenneth Copeland Ministries for a time. In 2013, we transitioned to Gateway Church, and I started my first healing journey through their Freedom Ministries courses created by Bob Hamp, LMFT.</div><div><br /></div><div>Throughout 2013-2016 I began a mental health journey, due to a mixture of lifelong sexual/relationship addiction, religious trauma, and autistic burn-out. In 2017, my wife and I had the best year of our marriage. In 2018 my wife died and I was left a single father. My eldest son Connor, 13 at the time, cried in the ER â€œWe prayed and believed, and mommy still died, the Bible is a liar!â€ â€“ Which set me on a path to answer some nagging questions for my family. In 2019 I enrolled at The Kingâ€™s University (TKU) and completed a Bachelors in General Christian Studies in December 2022. I worked with two skilled Christian mental health therapists to process addiction, grief, learn new coping skills, and develop healthier boundaries and communication methods. In the middle of all that, I met a fellow widow and remarried in April 2022.</div><div><br /></div><div>Thanks to great professors and to the work of scholars such as Drs. Michael Heiser, John Walton, NT Wright, Tim Mackie, Richard Bauckham, Mark Nanos, and others, I was able to learn to think better about the Ancient Near East (ANE) and Second Temple Judaism context of the biblical authors.</div><div>Today I meet with other folks who are Deconstructing and help them process their Bible questions, and I meet with fellow Reconstructionists to process the biblical texts together, learning how to do hermeneutics in community. I write at www.NoHiding.Faith where I process my journey from Deconstruction to Reconstruction.&nbsp;</div><div><br /></div><div>While I have received answers to all the early questions that drove me into Bible School, I was surprised to find I had fallen in love with this book we call â€œBibleâ€ all over again. Itâ€™s more profound than I first realized when I was reading it â€œliterallyâ€ and not â€œin contextâ€. I find myself reading biblical scholarship for fun, and I think itâ€™s time to get my official studies deeper.</div></div><div><br /></div><div><br /></div><div><h4 style="text-align: left;">Darrell's Statement of Purpose&nbsp;</h4><div><br /></div><div>Something profoundly shifted after becoming a Widower. Until then, every life event was simply one of many events, in a continuous cycle of events, but on June 25, 2018, everything changed. Career and money became meaningless, the life I had been building â€œwith herâ€ was gone forever. Business plans we had drafted were archived. All my values shifted to look for activities with deeper personal meaning. I stopped looking for the next promotion and instead started asking: â€œWhat am I curious about?â€&nbsp;</div><div><br /></div><div>After completing my BA, I spent time investigating all my options and weighing the costs. How much do I want to keep being involved in biblical studies? While I will never stop learning and growing, I no longer sit on the knifeâ€™s edge of doubt caused by my sonâ€™s ER queries. We know how to read the text in context now. Should I continue to pursue formal education, or read and study on my own?</div><div><br /></div><div>As I pondered these things, I found that I kept meeting new people who would ask about my biblical studies training. While the answers I give them seem basic to me, after swimming in this pond for years, those answers continue to intrigue the people I meet with. I found myself returning to all my study materials, looking up new scholarly articles for questions I hadnâ€™t considered, and genuinely enjoying the field of biblical scholarship on its own merit. I decided that blogging, writing, and one-on-one discussions are my canvas, and biblical scholarship is my paintbrush/toolkit.&nbsp;</div><div><br /></div><div>I want to see where else this road leads. Even if I never find a position teaching these things formally, in a school or a pulpit, even if I only ever use it in my private life and one-on-one, this is my passion subject, my Autistic special interest. This is what I want to do with the rest of my life, to play in this never ending well of biblical scholarship.</div><div><br /></div><div>As a Business Intelligence Analyst, I enjoy data analytics. Something I loved about the late Dr Michael Heiser was his reliance â€œon the dataâ€. I placed 80 programs from 57 schools into a spreadsheet and reviewed them for (1) the program requirements, (2) the capacity to spend some time getting further into ANE studies (3) but acknowledging that I still have a family to raise, (4) their ability to do the program online and (5) their theological commitments. Out of that batch, Fuller Theological Seminary proved to be the best option for my current path. It also came highly recommended by my former professor (a Fuller Alumni).</div><div><br /></div><div>I am a hybrid between <a href="https://www.theopedia.com/neo-evangelicalism" target="_blank">Neo-Evangelical</a> theology and a <a href="https://www.kesherjournal.com/article/let-the-real-israel-please-rise-defining-messianic-judaism-and-the-israel-of-god/" target="_blank">Messianic Judaism</a> theology, with a pull towards home church and Fresh Expressions.&nbsp; I believe Fuller Seminary is a school with solid scholars, with whom I can safely continue exploring the biblical contexts and begin to turn my attention toward what may come next. Which leads me to the Statement of Calling.</div></div><div><br /></div><div><br /></div><div><h4 style="text-align: left;">Darrell's Statement of Calling</h4><div>At one time in my life, I thought it was a privilege to have worked near some â€œbig namesâ€ in the church world. I volunteered at Kenneth Copeland Ministries and Eagle Mountain Church, sat in Jerry Savelleâ€™s church, sat in other big-tent namesâ€™ churches, and served pastors who knew those big names. I volunteered at Gateway Church under Robert Morris every weekend for years. It all felt like thatâ€™s where I was headed, to preach to the tens of thousands. Until I had a full-blown breakdown in 2016.</div><div>Due to the combination of my mental health journey, widowhood, becoming a late-diagnosed Autistic and ADHD (AuDHD), and my deconstruction-to-reconstruction journey, Iâ€™ve changed the names I consider myself privileged to know:</div><div><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>A counselor who left his full-time pastor role to return to his practice because thatâ€™s how he could help people change their â€œway of thinkingâ€ instead of just the thoughts they think.&nbsp;</li><li>Another counselor who left her church work to counsel people like me through grief and trauma, both in shelters and in private practice.&nbsp;</li><li>A missionary who started a bible school in India, and still travels there every year, staying months at a time, even though he and his wife are in their mid-eighties. That missionary worked in the Reagan White House, but now sees his real accomplishments every week in India.</li><li>A man who gave up job security to start a little church in downtown Coeur dâ€™Alene and then love an entire congregation of people through their deconstruction and into reconstruction, helping them to think well about the text. I still go there to visit as often as I can make it down there.</li><li>A man who meets with multiple men, every day, often 3-5 per day, because his entire calling in this season is to build relationships and walk along side of men; a man whoâ€™s teaching me to do the same one chance encounter at a time.</li><li>A woman who wants nothing more than to be a wife, mother, and to love on people as a restaurant server and a soap maker at farmers markets. She challenged me to consider how my positions (politically and theologically) represent the love of Jesus.</li><li>So many individuals have shown me how to love people into wholeness.</li></ul></div><div><br /></div><div>I donâ€™t know if I will ever have a â€œcareerâ€ in biblical studies, in a monetary sense. It may be that I keep my work as a Business Intelligence Analyst, building Excel workbooks into retirement as what my counselor called â€œmy tent making jobâ€.</div><div><br /></div><div>I want to start attending Society of Biblical Literature (SBL) conferences and possibly present some papers. I want to continue my journey into the world of biblical scholarship. I want to use that knowledge to help people think better about the text, and as a result think better about their relationship with Yeshua HaMashiach, Yahweh, and HaRuach HaKodesh, and as a result love the people around them a little better. Beyond that? Weâ€™ll see.</div></div><div><br /></div><div><br /></div><p></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">Join me on a NoHiding journey from Deconstruction --to-- Reconstruction</span></h3><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></div><h4 style="text-align: left;"><b><span style="font-family: inherit;">What's NoHiding.Faith about?<span>&nbsp;</span></span></b></h4><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit; font-weight: normal;">It's a phrase my late-wife and I started using after God healed our marriage. We learned that hiding emotions from each other led to separation while opening ourselves up to each other in vulnerability lead to becoming closer. So every time one of us would feel the other starting to shut-down, we could say "No Hiding" and it was a call to open back up and stay vulnerable, even and especially if it meant facing some really hard stuff together.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit; font-weight: normal;">After she died and I began deconstructing the Modern Western American Churchianity I grew up with, and Reconstructing a worldview centered on the message the biblical authors were actually telling, I had to make some equally hard decisions about whether I was ready to let go of some deeply held beliefs.&nbsp;</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit; font-weight: normal;">I had to accept that there is no Rapture in the Bible, at all. That was a tough one, but after a long and honest review of the topic, I accepted that it was never a biblical concept. Like dominoes, all the trappings of Churchianity fell down. For many, that's where it stops. They go through Deconstruction and leave Jesus behind. I was incapable of doing that.&nbsp;</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit; font-weight: normal;">I've known him. I've seen him with my own eyes in a vision as a child. I've interacted with the spiritual realm. I am just incapable of denying Jesus. But how do I separate all my traditions from the real deal?</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit; font-weight: normal;"><br /></span></p><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">"But my pastor says..."</span></h4><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">I threw&nbsp;</span><span style="font-family: inherit;">away many Christian Traditions, Denominations, Systematic Theologies, Creeds and medieval to modern concepts imposed on the biblical authors that those authors would never recognize... and I learned to ask:</span></div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;"></span></p><blockquote><span style="font-family: inherit;"><b><i>"What did the biblical authors want to communicate to their original audience (in their own original Ancient Near East and Second Temple Period contexts)?"</i>&nbsp;<i>Aka Exegesis.</i></b></span></blockquote><p></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;"><span><span>And I learned to ask that </span><b style="text-decoration-line: underline;">before</b>&nbsp;I can</span><span>&nbsp;ask:</span></span></p><p style="text-align: left;"></p><blockquote><span style="font-family: inherit;"><b><i>"What can the Bible teach me for my life today?"</i>&nbsp;Aka&nbsp;</b></span><b>Hermeneutics&nbsp;</b></blockquote><p></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">That should be simple, it should be what every Bible teacher does on every Sunday morning.&nbsp;</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">In my experience, at 100s of churches in three plus states, it isn't. Almost nobody does this.&nbsp;</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">Instead, we all get up and "preach" the traditions we learned to loosely base on the text by ripping sentences from their contexts and slapping them into our messages and bumper stickers are "proof" we are right about what we're saying (aka proof texting, which is a bad thing).</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">In fact, there is a division between Seminary training for most Pastoral roles and training for Academic roles. While there are exceptions, most pastors get far too little academic training and are never really trained well to think-well-about-the-text. They go on to lead business enterprises (Churchianity) but spend little to no time reviewing and reading and keeping up to date with biblical scholarship. For many, the last academic text they will read is in school. Their reading becomes mostly what other pastors are writing, or the latest celebrity pastor book. Most of these are non-academics.&nbsp;</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">I'm just not interested in traditions, maybe it's my autistic side.&nbsp;</span><span style="font-family: inherit;">As far as I'm concerned, Augustine was a man who loved Jesus and "wrestled with God" as we all do, each in our own generation. But there is no reason for me to place anything he said over anyone else. I'm as likely to find wisdom in the words of an illiterate man or woman who loves Jesus as I do in an ancient theologian. But thanks to modern scholarship, we can understand the biblical authors worldview (and therefore meaning) better than we ever have. Today, I would assign Augustine</span><span style="font-family: inherit;">&nbsp;far less weight than I would assign to modern biblical scholarship because we simply have tools he lacked.&nbsp;</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">I respect the journey and wrestling of an Augustine, Luther, Calvin, Wesley, or even a Billy Graham; but I don't have to accept their conclusions.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">It's time to bring fresh eyes to the text.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">I've joined the revolution, will you?&nbsp;</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></p><h4><span style="font-family: inherit;">Like the Rabbis I mentioned earlier, I love disagreement as long as its respectful; changing your mind is wonderful, do it often.</span></h4><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><i></i></span></div><blockquote><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><i>*Note: I am still growing out of my tendencies, taught to me by western Churchianity, to insist on "being right". As a result, I sometimes post an article, comment, or social media post that takes my conclusions too far or stands on them too firmly. I'm still growing in this. My aspirational self wants to allow room at the table for everyone's current ability to ask questions of the text and seek the biblical author's worldview. I fail to live up to this aspirational self. But I'm working on it, literally working with counselors and mentors on it. Call me out on this if you see it in me, I'll apologize after I cool off! Ha hah a..&nbsp;</i></span></div><div></div></blockquote><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">I love these Albertisms, because they apply to this journey so well:</span></div><div><ul><li><span style="font-family: inherit;">Strive not to be a success, but rather to be of value. Albert Einstein</span></li><li><span style="font-family: inherit;">If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it well enough. Albert Einstein</span></li><li><span style="font-family: inherit;">The difference between stupidity and genius is that genius has its limits. Albert Einstein</span></li><li><span style="font-family: inherit;">To admit that you were wrong is to declare that you are wiser now than you were before. Albert Einstein.</span></li></ul></div><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">I reserve the right to change my mind, in fact I almost guarantee it.&nbsp;</span></h4><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">Part of NoHiding is being open, raw, and taking an honest look at our worldview and changing our hearts and minds is that it results in changes to the way we see things. I have written things in years past that I passionately disagree with today. It's tough to face your deeply held beliefs and then let them go for something that is truer than what you once held true.</span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">All that being said, if you disagree with something you see or read here, that's OKAY! I might disagree with it too, tomorrow.&nbsp;</span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">Comment, share, <a href="https://linktr.ee/darrellwolfe" target="_blank">find me on social media</a>.&nbsp;</span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></div><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">The No Hiding Commitment:</span></h4><div><p></p><p><span style="font-family: inherit;">If you like the sound of this journey, make the following commitments to yourself and your community:<br /></span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: inherit;"><b>We are committed to study the text of the Bible for what it says, not what our traditions would tell us it says.</b></span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: inherit;"><b>We are committed to face hard truths, even if they require us to change our ideas and even more our character!</b></span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: inherit;"><b>We are committed to be honest, open, raw, real, and vulnerable in our relationship with God and relationships with each other. That means sometimes we have to say, "I don't know" or even"I was wrong".</b></span></li></ul><div><span><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></span></div><div><span><span style="font-family: inherit;">Let's do this journey together.</span><br /><br /><br /><i><b></b></i><blockquote><i><b>Disclosure <a href="https://chat.openai.com/?model=gpt-4" target="_blank">ChatGPT-4o</a>: While the vast majority of the words in this post are my own, I want to acknowledge that some of the phrasing and biblical text quotes in this blog post/article were generated with the help of ChatGPT-4o, a language model developed by OpenAI.</b></i>&nbsp;</blockquote><blockquote><i><b>I remain the author of the work, but ChatGPT-4o is the new Google.</b></i>&nbsp;<i><b>Where we would have once searched Google to find a reference or gain clarity on a point, we now use Google's replacement. The article was not written by ChatGPT-4o but was written with assistance from ChatGPT-4o.</b></i></blockquote><blockquote><div style="font-family: inherit;"></div></blockquote><div style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></div><div style="font-family: inherit;"><i>PS: I woke up this morning at 4:45am PST and started writing all of the thoughts that have been living in my head around this topic. I finished at 12:39pm PST. After 12,700 words, I feel I've barely scratched the surface of laying down the groundwork for understanding how different my understanding the of the biblical author's worldview has changed. I suspect many more articles in the future. Let this be the groundwork. June 9, 2024</i></div><br /><span style="font-family: inherit;">Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller</span></span></div><div><span style="font-family: inherit;">#NoHiding</span><p style="font-size: 11pt; margin: 0in;"><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /></span></p><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/no-hiding-logo-200-190-b8c91f03d9b9.png" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><span style="font-family: inherit;"><img border="0" data-original-height="190" data-original-width="200" height="196" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/no-hiding-logo-200-190-9eebe3837163.png" width="205" /></span></a></div><div><div id="mc_embed_signup"><form action="https://faith.us18.list-manage.com/subscribe/post?u=e52cc208090ca3637481b7c6e&amp;id=835af1e217" class="validate" id="mc-embedded-subscribe-form" method="post" name="mc-embedded-subscribe-form" novalidate="" target="_blank"><div id="mc_embed_signup_scroll"><div class="optionalParent"><div class="clear foot"></div></div></div></form></div><span style="font-family: inherit;"><script src="//s3.amazonaws.com/downloads.mailchimp.com/js/mc-validate.js" type="text/javascript"></script><script type="text/javascript">(function($) {window.fnames = new Array(); window.ftypes = new Array();fnames[0]='EMAIL';ftypes[0]='email';fnames[1]='FNAME';ftypes[1]='text';fnames[2]='LNAME';ftypes[2]='text';fnames[3]='ADDRESS';ftypes[3]='address';fnames[4]='PHONE';ftypes[4]='phone';fnames[5]='BIRTHDAY';ftypes[5]='birthday';}(jQuery));var $mcj = jQuery.noConflict(true);</script><!--End mc_embed_signup--></span><p><span style="font-family: inherit;"><br /><b><span style="font-size: large;">DISCLOURES</span></b></span></p><ul><li><i><span style="font-family: inherit;"><b>Endorse vs Recommend:</b>&nbsp;The Resources are recommended. That means I think they will help you in some way. That does NOT mean, I automatically agree with every word of every voice. I might recommend a book that comes to the wrong conclusion but presents good data for you to add to your library and thinking. In some cases, I do wholly endorse the individual but I probably still have minor disagreements with each of them on some point or another. We must learn to get away from being "I'm of Paul" "Well, I'm of Apollos" "Well, I'm of Peter". Nobody is recommended because you should believe every word they say, you shouldn't believe every word of what anyone says. You should use them as one more data source. The more sources of data one has, the better thinker they become.&nbsp;</span></i></li></ul><ul><li><i><span style="font-family: inherit;"><b>Affiliate</b>: This website includes several links to external websites, books, and products. Some, but not all, of these links may be "affiliate links". This means we may get paid for anything you buy when you click those links. Regardless, I only include links to items or places I found personally valuable or I have found helpful in solving people's problems and struggles. On that note, if you click on any Amazon link it helps this website, if you know you are going to buy from Amazon anyway, please click away.</span></i></li></ul><p></p><h4 style="clear: both;"><span style="font-family: inherit;">#NoHiding #Reconstruction &gt; #Deconstruction</span></h4><h4 style="clear: both;"><span style="font-family: inherit;"><span style="font-size: 11pt;">Â© Topos Creative LLC&nbsp;</span><span style="font-size: 11pt;">March 02, 2022</span></span></h4></div>

<p>  </p></div></div><p></p></div>
